summary: Shapiro recounts his attempt to build an end-grain butcher block cutting board from oak, only to discover that oak's extremely open pores make it unsuitable for food preparation — shellac soaked straight through the 1.75-inch board. He improvises a fix using diluted wood glue and powdered pumice to fill the pores, then sands it smooth. The post is a lighthearted woodworking cautionary tale that ties into the broader online debate about wood versus plastic cutting boards and food safety. His conclusion: just use maple.
key_points: Oak's open-pore structure makes it a poor choice for cutting boards — it acts like a wooden sponge that harbors bacteria
Shapiro improvised a pore-filling technique using diluted Titebond II glue, powdered pumice, and denatured alcohol to salvage the board
The wood-versus-plastic cutting board debate is a surprisingly contentious topic online, comparable to Linux-versus-Windows arguments
End-grain cutting boards are made by gluing strips, re-cutting perpendicular, rotating so grain faces up, and re-gluing — producing the characteristic small-square pattern
money_quotes: The subject approaches the ever-enlightening 'linux versus windows' debates for elucidating online reading.
It's like a water park for bacteria.
The shellac literally soaked through the 1.75" thick board and dripped out the other side.
Oak is basically a big wooden sponge, and as such not exactly suitable for a sterile chopping block.
Pumice: it's not just for Pompeiians any more.
If you see posts dated after that, the filling process worked and I don't have salmonella.
Just start with maple. You'll thank me for it.
key_insight: Choosing the right material matters more than clever fixes — oak's open pores make it fundamentally wrong for cutting boards, and no amount of improvisation fully substitutes for starting with the right wood.
